text: Although bimetallic iron-metal-nitrogen-carbon (FeMNC) catalysts have recently shown enhanced activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic media, the exact nature of their active sites and the synergistic interactions between the two metals remain elusive. In this study, we performed a comparative structural and catalytic analysis of FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts relative to their monometallic FeNC and SnNC counterparts. CO cryo-chemisorption measurements indicated that the density of M-N<sub>x</sub> sites in FeSnNC and FeCoNC is nearly half that of the parent materials. Despite this reduction in site density, the mass activity of both bimetallic catalysts is 50–100% higher than that of FeNC, driven by a significantly higher turnover frequency. Electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy demonstrated the physical coexistence of Fe-N<sub>x</sub> and Sn-N<sub>x</sub> or Co-N<sub>x</sub> moieties, yet provided no evidence for the formation of binuclear Fe-M-N<sub>x</sub> active sites. <sup>57</sup>Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy further revealed that the introduction of a second metal shifts the distribution of iron environments, resulting in a higher D<sub>1</sub>/D<sub>2</sub> ratio of Fe-N<sub>x</sub> spectral signatures compared to the monometallic FeNC. These findings suggest that the secondary metal promotes the formation of D<sub>1</sub> sites, which are inherently more active and responsible for the enhanced turnover frequency observed in these bimetallic systems.
